story:
Detective Elijah Crain was Doverton’s hero. Sharp jaw. Sharper instincts. A gun, and a promise: he would stop the murders of young girls haunting the south side. The press called him the Watcher. The city believed him.

Sergeant Dana Rowen had worked with him before. Brilliant, relentless, and strange. She muttered case notes into a battered recorder, chewed butterscotch, and scribbled theories on napkins she treated like evidence. Some found it endearing. Crain did not.

When sixteen-year-old Emma Darcy vanished—ribbon in her hair, weekend shifts at the diner—Crain stared at her photo and felt the city’s hope land on his shoulders like a weight. Rowen clicked her recorder. Case Darcy. Likelihood of abduction: high. She unwrapped a candy and offered him one. He refused.

Days later, Emma was found near the river, blue-faced, ribbon untied. At the morgue, the coroner spoke quietly. Cause of death: obstruction of the airway. He lifted something out with forceps. A half-dissolved butterscotch. The coroner said it could be a tragic accident. It happens. Crain heard only the wet clink on the tray. He looked at Rowen, chewing her candy, unbothered.

That night, Martin Nelson, the quiet night guard with keys like a rosary, found Crain hollow-eyed. He spoke softly, almost shy. Kids shouldn’t die like that. And the candy—almost as if someone forced it in. He smiled, apologetic. Rowen gives me one every night. Guess it’s her thing. The comment slid into Crain’s chest and stayed there.

Then Leah Mercer disappeared. No body. No leads. Rowen kept recording, handing out sweets to rookies, to Martin at the door, to anyone who looked like they needed something to hold. Crain watched the ritual and saw a weapon. He pictured Leah in the dark, breath smothered by sugar.

Weeks later, another body washed up by the river. Crain stumbled through the tape, kneeling in the mud. He pried the girl’s mouth open with his fingers, frantic, bloody, shouting. It’s there—it has to be—the butterscotch—Rowen— He clawed and raved while cameras flashed. Rowen stood pale, silent, recorder in hand. Desecration, someone said. Insanity, the papers wrote.

By winter, the Watcher was gone. Crain was suspended, then jailed pending charges. The headlines were unkind. Detective Gone Mad. The Watcher Becomes the Watched. Rowen testified—odd, yes, but steady. The city judged Crain’s obsession, not a conspiracy, as the cause of his fall.

Only one man came out of that autumn with his reputation burnished: Martin Nelson, the night guard who never left his post. He spoke gently at vigils, carried Emma’s coffin when her father collapsed, turned keys in the station halls while the department crumbled. The features called him the man who stayed. Doverton loved him for it.

One quiet evening, after another careful interview, Martin walked home, whistling. He unlocked his basement door, easy and unhurried. In the dark below, Leah Mercer lifted her head.
